actions.thought: In a Heads-Up scenario, the Button acts first pre-flop. I hold Q3o, which is a very marginal hand, hovering near the bottom of the playable range even for a wide Button opening strategy. While being in position is advantageous, Q3o suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds (dominated Queen). Since this is the first hand and I have no information on the opponent's tendencies (whether they over-defend or 3-bet aggressively), the most prudent low-variance play is to fold. I prefer to start the match entering pots with hands that have better connectivity or high-card strength.

In a heads-up situation, Q4o is a strong enough hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. I have position for the rest of the hand, which is a significant advantage. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take initiative and build the pot against the Big Blind.

I hold ATo in the Big Blind, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up match, well above the median winning hand. Facing a standard 3x raise from the Button, folding is incorrect due to the strength of my hand. While 3-betting is a viable option to extract value or deny equity, it risks facing a 4-bet which would force a fold of a hand with good playability. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide and realize my equity, despite being out of position. Therefore, I will flat call the raise.

I am out of position as the pre-flop caller on a Q-3-4 board with a flush draw present. My hand is Ace-high (AsTh), which has some showdown value and two overcards. Standard strategy in heads-up play dictates checking to the pre-flop raiser to see their action. Donk betting here is unnecessary and unbalanced. I will check to evaluate the opponent's continuation bet strategy.

I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Fours) on a board with a flush draw. This is a very strong hand, and I need to bet for value and to charge any drawing hands like diamonds or straight draws. A bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot (400 chips) is appropriate to build the pot and protect my equity.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on a Q-4-3 board with two diamonds. I missed the flop completely, holding only Ace-high with two overcards. The opponent has made a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 600 (two-thirds pot). Being out of position without a flush draw or significant straight potential makes it difficult to realize equity. The bet size is large enough to discourage a light float. While I beat pure bluffs, I am well behind value hands and draws, and reverse implied odds are a concern if I hit an Ace against a hand like AQ. Folding is the prudent play.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the player on the Button (Small Blind) should play a very wide range of hands due to the positional advantage post-flop. 78o (Seven-Eight offsuit) is a connected hand that falls well within a standard opening range (top 80-90% of hands). Since I folded my previous button (Hand #1), I want to assert aggression and not allow the Big Blind to see a flop for free or cheaply every time. A min-raise to 200 puts pressure on the opponent, potentially taking down the pot pre-flop, while keeping the price of the steal attempt low. If called, I have a hand with straight potential and position to outmaneuver the opponent.
Defending the big blind with 65o against a 2x raise from the button. The pot odds are favorable (3:1) for a call in a heads-up game to see a flop.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a King-high board with a potential flush draw. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, checking to the aggressor is the standard play here. This allows me to control the pot size and see how 'Pro' reacts to the texture, as the King likely favors their range.

I hold middle pair (Eights) on a wet board (K-8-6 with two clubs). While I have showdown value, my hand is not strong enough to comfortably value bet for three streets, and I want to avoid being check-raised, which would force me to fold equity. The King hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or call down if the runout is safe. It is a standard pot-control line with a marginal made hand in position.

The turn 8s pairs the board, giving me two pair (8s and 6s). After the flop was checked through, it is less likely that 'Pro' has a strong King, though they could be trapping or have a medium-strength hand. By checking, I am controlling the pot and allowing 'Pro' to bet with a wider, potentially weaker range, or I can see a river for free if they check back.

On the Turn, the board pairs with the 8s, improving my hand to Trip Eights. However, my kicker (7) is relatively weak. In this spot, I am likely either way ahead of hands like Kx and flush draws, or dominated by a better Eight (A8, K8, T8, etc.). Betting opens me up to a check-raise which puts me in a difficult spot, potentially forcing me to fold significant equity. By checking back, I disguise the strength of my hand, control the pot size to avoid paying off a better kicker, and give the opponent an opportunity to bluff on the River with their missed draws. This line maximizes my ability to realize equity and catch bluffs while minimizing losses against superior hands.

On the river, the board is 8d-Kc-6c-8s-Qd. I have two pair, eights and sixes, with a five kicker. Pro has checked back both the flop and the turn after being the pre-flop aggressor, which suggests they likely do not have a King or a strong pocket pair. However, the Queen on the river is an overcard to my sixes and could have improved a hand like QJ or QT. Given the showdown value of my hand and the fact that most hands that would call a bet likely beat me (any Q, any K, or any 8), checking is the most prudent play to reach a cheap showdown.

I hold Trip Eights on a board of [8d Kc 6c 8s Qd]. While my hand has significant showdown value, the board runout presents several dangers. The Queen on the river counterfeits my kicker (my 7 doesn't play against the board's King and Queen), meaning I chop with most other 8s but lose to A8, and now lose to any Full Houses (K8, Q8, 66, QQ) or the JT straight. As established in my previous reasoning, betting opens me up to a check-raise which would put me in a difficult spot, potentially forcing me to fold the best hand or pay off a monster. Given the opponent's triple check, they likely hold a King, a weak pair, or a missed draw. By checking back, I secure the pot against weaker hands and chop with similar trips without risking a polarized confrontation against a boat or straight.

I am on the button with 2h 3h, which is a suited hand with some connectivity. In heads-up play, the button (Small Blind) should open with a wide range of hands to maintain pressure and capitalize on position. Given the previous hands, I want to establish an aggressive image. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 8c facing a 3bb raise (300 total) from the Button. J8s is a respectable hand in heads-up play, easily strong enough to defend. I need to call 200 into a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. Against a standard opening range, J8s performs well enough to justify a call. 3-betting is an option but calling allows me to realize equity with a hand that plays reasonably well post-flop without bloating the pot out of position.

On an As 8d Ts board, the texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash), who contains all strong Ax and Broadway hands in their range. Out of position with bottom pair (8s), I have some showdown value but no incentive to lead (donk bet). My goal is to check to the aggressor. If they check back, I realize equity for free. If they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, as bottom pair is often a check-call in Heads-Up play on the flop.

The flop comes As-8d-Ts. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture heavily favors my perceived range, as I am more likely to have strong Aces, Tens, or pocket pairs. Pro's check indicates weakness or a marginal hand. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to capitalize on my positional advantage and fold equity, representing a strong hand like an Ace or a flush draw.

I hold Jc8c on a board of As 8d Ts, giving me bottom pair. The opponent (Flash) has c-bet 300 (half pot) into a pot of 600. Heads-up, folding bottom pair to a single standard c-bet is generally too exploitable, as opponents c-bet this texture frequently with a wide range including draws (spades, straight draws like KQ/QJ) and air. I need 25% equity to call (300 to win 1200). Even against a range of strong Ax/Tx hands mixed with bluffs, my equity usually exceeds that threshold. Raising makes little sense as it isolates me against better hands. The best play is to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the 3c is a complete brick that does not change the board texture or complete any draws. The board remains As 8d Ts 3c. I still hold bottom pair with a decent kicker (J8). Since I called the flop c-bet out of position, leading (donking) the turn makes little sense as it folds out the opponent's bluffs and isolates me against their value range (Ax, Tx, sets). My goal is to check and evaluate. If the opponent checks back, I realize equity and see a river. If they bet again, I will have a tougher decision based on sizing, as a second barrel on this texture often signifies strength, but for now, checking is the standard flow.

The turn 3c gives me bottom pair, which provides some showdown value but is still quite weak against Pro's range after they called the flop c-bet. Pro's call on the As-8d-Ts flop suggests they likely have a piece of the board (Ax, Tx, 8x) or a draw (spades, J9). Since betting is unlikely to fold out better hands like a Ten or an Ace, and I want to keep the pot small with my marginal hand, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and potentially see a cheap showdown or improve on the river.

On the River, the board is As 8d Ts 3c Ad. I hold Jc 8c, giving me two pair (Aces and Eights) with a Jack kicker. The opponent checked back the turn, which often indicates pot control with a marginal hand (weak Ax, Tx) or a give-up with a draw/air. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (missed draws fold, lower pairs might fold) and isolates me against better hands (Ax, Tx, better 8x, overpairs). Checking is the best option to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bluff from missed draws (spades, straight draws) that arrived with no value. My hand has sufficient showdown value to win against bluffs and some lower pairs but is not strong enough to value bet.

The river brings a second Ace, pairing the board and giving me two pair (Aces and Threes). Pro has checked twice (turn and river) after calling my flop continuation bet. On this board (As-8d-Ts-3c-Ad), any Ten or Eight in Pro's range beats my pair of Threes. However, my hand does have showdown value against missed draws like J9 or spade draws that didn't materialize. There is very little value in betting here, as better hands (like a Ten, an Eight, or a mid-pocket pair) are unlikely to fold to a single river bet, and worse hands will simply fold. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and see the showdown for free in a pot that hasn't grown since the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 9s, a very weak off-suit hand (bottom 20% of range). Although I have the button and position post-flop, this hand has poor playability due to lack of connectivity and suitedness. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, 95o is usually a fold or a very low-frequency limp. Given I folded Q3o in Hand 1, maintaining a consistent range by folding the bottom trash hands is prudent to preserve chips for better spots. Folding is the standard decision here.
6c 8c is a strong suited hand for heads-up play from the button. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take initiative and build the pot.

In heads-up play, A6o is a respectable hand to defend against a button open. The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB). I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring roughly 25% equity. A6o has significantly more equity than this against a standard wide heads-up opening range. While a 3-bet is possible, calling is the standard line to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position. I will defend my big blind.

I am out of position on the flop with Ace-high (Ac 6d) on a board of Qd-2d-8s. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor who has more strong Queens and overpairs in their range. My hand has some showdown value and a weak backdoor flush draw, but it is not strong enough to lead out (donk bet). The standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. If they bet, I will decide whether to call or fold based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the correct move.

I have middle pair (8s) on a board with one overcard (Qd) and a flush draw (diamonds). My opponent checked to me. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws or overcards. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to maintain initiative and gather information about the strength of Pro's hand.

I hold Ace-Six offsuit (with the Six of Diamonds) on a board of Queen-Deuce-Eight with two diamonds. I checked the flop, and the opponent has made a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot). While I hold Ace-high which is ahead of pure bluffs, I am out of position and my hand has poor playability. I only have a weak backdoor flush potential and three outs to top pair (Ace), which could still be dominated. The opponent's range hits this board reasonably well (Queens, Eights, Flush Draws). Calling out of position with just Ace-high and a bad kicker against a half-pot bet is generally a losing play in the long run without a specific read that the opponent over-bluffs. I am getting 4:1 odds, but realizing that equity is difficult. Conserving chips is prudent here.

I am on the button with Q6o. In heads-up play, this hand is within the top 53% of hands and is strong enough to open for a raise, especially to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My opponent has been aggressive, so limping might invite a raise I'd have to fold to or call out of position with a marginal hand. A min-raise to 200 allows me to take the initiative. If I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold. Since I have folded 2 of my last 3 button opportunies, I need to open to maintain a balanced frequency.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 200. I will 3-bet to 600 to take the initiative, define their hand range, and build the pot with a hand that has strong broadway potential. This sizing puts pressure on their wide button-opening range while still being a manageable portion of my stack.

I am facing a 3-bet to 600 after opening with Q6o. While I have position post-flop (as the Button/SB in heads-up play), Q6o is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by my opponent's 3-betting range (which likely includes better Queens, Ax hands, and pairs). The pot odds require 33% equity, and Q6o often fails to realize its equity against a strong range. My pre-flop plan was explicitly to fold to a 3-bet, and without specific reads that the opponent is bluffing excessively, folding is the disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

K3o is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play. I have been raising to 300 in previous orbits from this position and will maintain that consistency to apply pressure and utilize my positional advantage. Pro has shown a willingness to defend but has folded to post-flop aggression or 3-bets, so a standard open is appropriate here.

I hold QJs, which is a premium hand in heads-up play, ranking well within the top 15-20% of hands. The opponent has opened to 3bb (300), a sizing they have used consistently. While QJs is strong enough to 3-bet for value, calling is the preferred line here for several reasons: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, including dominated hands like QTo or JTo. 2) Being out of position, keeping the pot manageable (SPR ~16) allows for better post-flop maneuverability. 3) The opponent has shown a tendency to c-bet frequently, providing opportunities to check-raise or check-call on favorable textures. I am getting 2:1 pot odds, requiring 33% equity, and QJs has significantly higher equity against a standard button opening range.

On a board of Ah 3c Kd, the texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash), who retains the range advantage with strong Ax and Kx hands. As the pre-flop caller out of position, I should check almost my entire range here. Although I hold a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and a backdoor flush draw with Qd Jd, leading out (donk betting) is not advisable. I will check to see if the opponent continues their aggression.

On a board of Ah-3c-Kd, I have flopped two pair (Kings and Threes). This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and my opponent checked, a value bet is the most logical step. This board favors my perceived range (which includes many Ax and Kx hands), and I want to get value from weaker King-X hands, Ace-X hands, or gutshot straight draws like QJ. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to build the pot while keeping the opponent's range wide enough to call.

I am facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) on an Ah 3c Kd board. The board texture heavily favors the opponent's range, as they likely have many Ax and Kx combos. However, I hold Qd Jd, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Ten) and a backdoor flush draw. I need to call 300 to win a total pot of 1200, offering me 4:1 pot odds (20% equity required to break even, though typically 25% is a safer target considering realized equity OOP). My hand has approximately 4 clean outs for the straight, plus backdoor flush potential and some pair outs against pure bluffs. Against a wide button opening and c-betting range, my equity is likely around 25-30%. Folding a broadway gutshot with backdoor flush potential for this price in a heads-up match is too tight. Raising is ill-advised as the opponent connects too well with this board. Therefore, I will call.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 3s, making it Ah 3c Kd 3s. Crucially, this card gives me a flush draw (diamonds) to go along with my gutshot straight draw. I now have significantly increased equity (approx. 12 outs: 9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond tens). Despite this, I am still out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who has shown strength on an Ace-King high board. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much as better hands (Ax, Kx) will call, and I fold out bluffs I want to keep in. The standard line is to check to the raiser. If they check back, I get a free card to hit my big draw. If they bet, I likely have the odds to call or potentially check-raise semi-bluff depending on sizing.

On the turn, the 3s completes my full house (Threes full of Kings). My hand is exceptionally strong, and the board texture (A-K-3-3) still allows my opponent to have several hands they might call with, such as any Ace or King. Since my opponent checked to me again, I will continue to bet for value. A bet of 600 (half the pot) is large enough to build the pot while remaining enticing for an Ace or a weaker King to call or potentially check-raise.

I hold QdJd on a board of Ah 3c Kd 3s. The opponent has fired a second barrel of 600 into a pot of 1200. Upon reviewing the board carefully, I note that contrary to the previous internal monologue, I do not have a flush draw; there is only one diamond on the board (Kd) and two in my hand, totaling three. My only draw is a gutshot straight draw needing a Ten for the Broadway straight. This gives me 4 outs, which equates to approximately 9% equity. The pot odds offered are 3:1 (requiring 25% equity). Since 9% is well below the required 25%, and I am out of position against an opponent showing strength on an Ace-King high board, I do not have the right price to call. Floating to bluff the river is risky and unprofitable in this spot. Therefore, the correct play is to fold.

I hold 3c 7h in the Small Blind (Button). This is a 7-high offsuit hand, which ranks near the bottom of all starting hands. While heads-up strategy dictates playing a wide range from the button, 73o is extremely weak. My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression, including a 3-bet in a previous hand when I opened from the button. Opening this hand invites a difficult post-flop situation or forces a fold to a 3-bet. Conserving chips by folding the bottom of my range is the prudent decision here.

I am holding pocket nines (9s 9h) on the button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to extract value and build a pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the big blind while maintaining a strong range representation.

I hold Pocket Jacks, a premium hand in Heads-Up play. The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button, which is a consistent opening size for them. I want to build the pot immediately for value and charge weaker hands or floats, rather than playing passively out of position. A 3-bet is the standard play here. I will raise to 1100, which is approximately 3.6x the opening bet, a suitable size for being out of position with effective stacks around 90-100BBs.

I have pocket nines, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Pro has 3-bet my open to 1100. While a 4-bet is an option, calling allows me to play a controlled pot in position and see how the flop develops. Pocket nines play well against a 3-betting range that includes many high-card combinations and smaller pairs. By calling, I keep his bluffs in and can leverage my position post-flop.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jd) on a board of 6c Ts 9d. The pot is 2200 after I 3-bet pre-flop and was called. This board texture interacts heavily with the opponent's calling range, which likely includes tens (AT, KTs), sets (TT, 99, 66), and straight draws (QJ, 87, 78). While I have an overpair, my hand is vulnerable. Looking at the opponent's history (Hands 2, 4, 6, 8), they consistently bet ('raise to') when checked to on the flop. Therefore, checking is the optimal play here to: 1) Protect my checking range (which contains missed overcards like AK/AQ), 2) Induce a bluff or a value bet from a worse hand (Tx) from the aggressive opponent, and 3) Avoid facing a raise on this wet board which would put me in a difficult spot. I intend to check-call.

I have flopped a set of nines (middle set), which is a powerhouse on this 6-T-9 board. Pro 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked to me. While Pro's check could be a trap with an overpair (JJ-AA) or TT, it's more likely he has missed with hands like AK/AQ or is playing cautiously with a marginal hand. However, given the straight draws present (QJ, 87, J8), I need to bet for value and to protect my hand. A bet of 1100 (half pot) is large enough to extract value from overpairs and draws while keeping his range wide enough to continue.

I hold an overpair (JJ) on a Ts 9d 6c board. The opponent has bet 1100 (half-pot) after I checked. As analyzed previously, the opponent is aggressive and likely to bet when checked to. My hand is currently ahead of their top pair range (AT, KT) and draws, but behind sets or two pair. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me or strong draws, effectively isolating myself against a stronger range. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue with worse made hands or bluffs. The Stack-to-Pot Ratio is manageable, so I will call to see a turn card.

The turn is the 3s, which is a brick that does not significantly change the board texture or complete the obvious draws (QJ, 87). The pot is 4400 and my remaining stack is 7050 (SPR ~1.6). I have an overpair (JJ) which is a strong bluff-catcher in this spot. Having checked and called the flop, leading out on a blank turn doesn't make much sense; it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands (sets, two pair). Checking is the standard play to protect my checking range and allow the aggressive opponent to potentially barrel with draws (QJ, flush draws) or value bet worse hands (Tx). If the opponent bets again, I will have to decide based on sizing, but my hand is likely strong enough to continue given the SPR.

I have a set of nines (middle set) on a 6c-Ts-9d-3s board. Pre-flop, I called a 3-bet and on the flop, my opponent (Pro) led out for 1100, which I called to keep their range wide and potentially trap. The turn is a 3s, which is a blank unless Pro has exactly pocket threes (unlikely given the pre-flop 3-bet). Pro has now checked to me. The pot is 4400. I have a monster hand and need to continue building the pot to target their stack. Pro's range likely consists of overpairs (JJ-AA) or high equity draws like QJ. By betting here, I can get value from those hands. I will bet 2200, which is exactly half the pot, to set up a potential river shove while leaving enough room for them to call or check-raise with a worse hand.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jd) on a turn board of 6c Ts 9d 3s. The turn card is a blank that does not complete the obvious straight draws (QJ, 87) from the flop. The opponent has bet 2200 (half-pot) after calling a pre-flop 3-bet and betting the flop. Their range is polarized between sets/two-pair (which beat me), top pair hands like AT/KT (which I beat), and semi-bluffs/draws. Given the half-pot sizing, I am getting good odds (need ~25% equity) to call against a range that includes many worse hands and bluffs. Folding an overpair here would be too exploitable, and raising would only isolate me against superior hands. The best play is to call and re-evaluate on the river.

I hold Pocket Jacks on a board of 6c Ts 9d 3s Kc. The pot is significant (8800) and I have 4850 remaining. The River King is a terrible card for my hand. It completes the obvious QJ open-ended straight draw from the flop and puts an overcard on the board that interacts well with a pre-flop 3-bet calling range (KQ, KJs, KTs). At this point, my hand beats only missed draws like 87 or random floats. Leading out (shoving) would be disastrous as worse hands will fold and better hands (Kx, straights, sets, two pair) will call. My best option is to check and hope the opponent checks back with a hand like AT or a missed draw. If they shove, I will likely have to fold given the texture change, but checking is the only strategic option to minimize loss or realize equity.

Flash holds a set of nines on a board of 6c-Ts-9d-3s-Kc. There are no possible straights or flushes. Flash has been the aggressor on the flop and turn, and Pro has check-called both streets before checking the river. Pro's range likely consists of overpairs such as AA, QQ, and JJ, or a top-pair hand like AK which improved on the river. While TT or KK would beat Flash, they represent a small portion of Pro's range. Given the pot size of 8800 and Pro's remaining stack of 4850, Flash should move all-in to extract maximum value from Pro's overpairs and top pairs.

The river card is the King of Clubs (Kc), which is a very bad card for my hand (Pocket Jacks). It completes the obvious QJ straight draw from the flop and improves Kx hands (like KQ, KTs) that would call a pre-flop 3-bet to top pair or two pair. While holding two Jacks blocks some QJ combinations, I am now behind almost all value hands in the opponent's range, including sets (TT, 99, 66), straights, and any King. The opponent has taken an extremely strong line (triple barrel in a 3-bet pot), culminating in an all-in shove. Given that the King favors my perceived range (AK, AA, KK) yet the opponent is still shoving, their range is likely very strong. My hand is merely a bluff catcher that loses to a significant portion of their value range. I have better hands to call with in this spot (AK, AA, KK, sets). Preserving my remaining stack is crucial.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 8d 4c, a very weak off-suit hand (84o). I am currently at a significant chip disadvantage (48 BBs vs 150 BBs) after losing a large pot in the previous hand. My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression, frequently raising pre-flop and barreling post-flop. Playing a trash hand like 84o out of position (if called) or facing a 3-bet would be -EV. Preserving my stack for better spots is the prudent strategy here. Therefore, I will fold.

I am on the button with a suited Queen, Qh 3h. Against a short-stacked opponent with only 47 BBs, I want to apply pressure. A standard open raise to 300 is effective here to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My hand has decent post-flop playability due to its suit.

The opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 3x (300). I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4d. While suited connectors/gappers have potential, 74s is on the weaker side. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), requiring ~33% realized equity. However, I will be playing out of position (OOP) post-flop against an aggressive opponent who has a significant chip lead and has been applying pressure. Being OOP makes it difficult to realize full equity, likely dropping my realized equity below the required threshold against a 3x open. Given my dwindling stack (47BB) and the larger raise size, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am dealt 7d 2h (72o) on the Button/Small Blind, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of 46.5 BB, I need to be selective, especially against an aggressive opponent like Flash who holds a significant chip lead and has shown a willingness to 3-bet and barrel post-flop. Attempting to steal the blinds or limp with this hand has negative expected value. The disciplined and correct play is to simply fold and preserve chips for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Pro, has a relatively short stack of 4550 compared to my 15300. By raising to 300, I exert pressure, maintain initiative, and leverage my positional advantage. This size is standard for building the pot while keeping risk manageable if they decide to shove or re-raise.

I hold A9o in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the opponent's likely wide opening range. The opponent has raised to 300 (3x BB), a sizing they have used consistently. Given my stack depth of roughly 45 BBs and the opponent's demonstrated aggression (frequent c-bets and multi-street barrels), I prefer to call rather than 3-bet. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable out of position and keep the opponent's range wide, whereas 3-betting might isolate me against better hands or force me to fold to a 4-bet. I'm getting 2:1 odds on a call, which is very profitable for A9o.

I hold Ac9s on a board of Td Qd 9c. I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. The board is very wet, connecting well with the opponent's pre-flop raising range (containing many Broadway cards). As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or strong draws. My opponent has shown a high frequency of c-betting, so checking allows me to see their action and decide whether to call or fold based on their bet sizing, while keeping the pot smaller with a marginal hand.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Five offsuit and was called. The flop of Ten-Queen-Nine is very coordinated and hits a lot of the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws). As the pre-flop aggressor, I have Ace-high. My opponent checked to me. While I could continuation bet to try and take the pot down, this board is so connected that they are unlikely to fold any piece of it. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free, potentially improve to an Ace, or evaluate the action on the next street without bloating the pot with a weak holding on a dangerous board.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Nines) on the turn, but the board texture is extremely wet with three diamonds (Td Qd Ad) and straight possibilities (K-J). The opponent's check-back on the flop suggests they likely didn't have a strong hand or draw at that point, perhaps holding Ace-high or a weak pair. The Turn Ace hits the Button's opening range hard. Checking is the prudent play here: it allows the opponent to bet their Ax hands for value (which I beat) or bluff with air, while preventing me from bloating the pot out of position against a potential flush or straight. If I lead, I likely only get called by better hands or strong draws.

I turned top pair with an Ace on a very wet board (Td-Qd-9c-Ad). While my kicker (5d) is weak, it is the five of diamonds, giving me a nut flush draw to go with my top pair. I hold two pair (Aces and Nines) on a wet board (Td Qd 9c Ad). I hold Two Pair (Aces and Nines) on a dangerous board containing four diamonds (Td Qd Ad 4d). Any single diamond in the opponent's hand completes a flush, and a straight (KJ) is also possible. The risk of getting 3-bet or having to fold to aggression post-flop makes investing chips with T4o negative EV.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent is relatively short-stacked with only 18.5 big blinds remaining. I will continue my aggressive strategy and open with a standard 3x raise to put pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o and 18.5 BBs (1850 chips). The opponent (Button) raised to 300 (3bb). I need to call 200 more to win a total pot of 600, giving me 3:1 odds (need 33% equity). K7o has approximately 43-47% equity against a standard Button opening range, making a call +EV. Shoving 18.5 BBs with K7o is slightly over-aggressive and high variance given the 3x open size which reduces fold equity slightly compared to a min-raise. Folding is too tight given the price. I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position on a paired board [6c 6d 4c] with King-high. The standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) does not accomplish much as worse hands fold and better hands call or raise. My hand has some showdown value and backdoor potential with the King of clubs, so I intend to check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I hold Ace-high on a paired board of 6-6-4. My opponent is extremely short-stacked with only 1,650 chips remaining. While this board can occasionally hit a big blind's range, my Ace-high is currently the best hand against most of my opponent's air. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and try to take down the pot immediately or force my opponent to commit a significant portion of their stack to continue.

I am out of position with 16.5 BBs facing a half-pot c-bet from an aggressive opponent who bets this spot frequently. While K-high technically has equity against a wide pure-bluff range on this paired board, I lack a pair or strong draw. My hand (K7o with Kc) has a weak backdoor flush draw and two overcards to the 4, but calling 300 chips (nearly 20% of my remaining stack) is expensive for a marginal bluff-catcher. If I call and face aggression on the turn, I will likely have to fold, wasting chips. Preserving my 1650 stack gives me better fold equity for future pre-flop shoves. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Current stack is 16 BB (1600 chips). Hand J5s is a marginal hand, sitting around the 43rd percentile of hands. Given the opponent's massive chip lead and recent tendency to 3-bet my button raises (e.g., Hand #7, #27), opening with a weak hand is dangerous. Shoving 16 BB with J5s is negative EV (usually a fold above ~10 BB in Nash charts). Min-raising exposes me to a 3-bet which I must fold to, wasting chips. Limping is an option but leaves me vulnerable to aggression. The best play is to preserve the stack for a stronger holding to shove or raise with.

I am on the button with Ac 9d, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. The opponent, Pro, is very short-stacked with only 15 big blinds remaining. Raising here puts immediate pressure on their stack and allows me to either take down the blinds or see a flop with a strong hand. I will continue my established pattern of opening for 3x the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with a very short stack of 15 BBs (1500 chips). My hand, 5h 2c, is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. My opponent raised to 300. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), the equity of 52o is poor (around 29% vs random, less vs a raising range), and its playability out of position is terrible. With such a short stack, I cannot afford to bleed chips in marginal or losing spots. Preserving my stack for a better shoving or defending opportunity is the correct strategy.

I hold AKo, a premium hand, on the Button with a short stack of 14.5 BBs. My opponent 'Flash' has a massive chip lead and has demonstrated a frequent tendency to 3-bet against my pre-flop raises (observed in hands 7, 27, 41, 55). Shoving all-in immediately is profitable but might fold out weaker hands that Flash would otherwise defend or bluff with. By min-raising to 200, I invite Flash to call with a wider range or, ideally, 3-bet, allowing me to get my stack in as a heavy favorite. If Flash calls, I have position and a strong hand to navigate the post-flop. This line maximizes expected value against an aggressive big stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit. The opponent, Pro, is short-stacked with only 1300 chips (13 BB) and has raised to 200 (2 BB) from the Button/Small Blind. To call, I need to put in 100 more chips into a current pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 pot odds. In a heads-up match, J2o has enough equity (roughly 35-40% against a reasonable opening range) to justify calling 100 chips to see a flop, especially since I have a massive chip lead and can afford the small investment to potentially eliminate or further cripple the short stack if I hit the board hard.

I have Jack-high and have completely missed this paired flop. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, who is very short-stacked, I will check to see how they react. I want to avoid committing more chips with a weak hand against a range that contains many Queens and pocket pairs.

I hold Ks Ah on a dry, paired board of Qs 5d 5h. With 13 BBs left, pot control is crucial. While I have the best unfilled hand (Ace-high), I lose to any pair. Betting here likely only folds out worse hands (lower unpaired hands) and gets called by better (pairs, trips). Checking back allows me to realize my equity (6 outs to top pair) for free, keeps the pot manageable, and potentially induces bluffs from the opponent on later streets which I can pick off. I preserve my stack for a better spot or a hit on the turn.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high. The board is Qs-5d-5h-6d. After calling a pre-flop raise, the flop went check-check. I have no pair and no real draw. Pro, the Button/Small Blind, is extremely short-stacked with only 1300 chips remaining. Since I have no hand value and limited equity, betting into a short stack who might feel committed with any Ace-high or marginal piece is unwise. I will check to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if the board runs out favorably or if the opponent also has a very weak hand.

I hold Ace-King high on a board of Qs-5d-5h-6d. My stack is short at 13 BBs. After checking back the flop, the turn brings a 6d, adding a flush draw to the board. My opponent checks to me again. Betting here achieves little: better hands (pairs) will call, and worse hands (like completely missed broadways) will fold, though I might get protection against equity from random live cards or draws. However, if I bet and face a check-raise, I am forced to fold a hand that has significant equity (6 outs to top pair) and potentially currently the best hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot manageable relative to my stack, and takes a hand with decent showdown value to the river. I plan to re-evaluate on the river, potentially calling a small bet or checking back to win with Ace-high.

I have Jack-high on a board that contains two overcards (Queen and King) and a pair of fives. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked both the flop and the turn, which suggests a weak range or a marginal hand like Ace-high. With my current chip lead (18,300 vs 1,300) and no showdown value, I have little to gain by betting into a short stack who might feel priced in to call with any small pair or Ace-high. Checking is the most prudent play to see if my hand can win at showdown without risking more chips in a small pot.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with an Ace kicker on a board of Qs-5d-5h-6d-Kd. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. The river King is a scare card that completes a backdoor flush draw and puts an overcard to the Queen. My opponent checked the river. While I likely have the best hand, it is difficult to get called by worse hands (like Qx or pocket pairs) because the King is a significant scare card for their range. Betting risks facing a check-raise or folding out all the hands I beat while only getting called by better (trips/flush) or a chop. Given my short stack (13 BB) and the desire to minimize variance while locking up the pot, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, especially against a short-stacked opponent. Pro only has 1600 chips remaining (16 big blinds), while I have a massive lead. I want to put pressure on their stack by raising. A standard 3x raise to 300 is usually effective, but since Pro is short-stacked, I'll stick to a consistent opening size of 300 to keep the pressure on.

I am in the Big Blind with 16 BBs (1600 chips) holding A4o. My opponent, Flash, has been opening nearly 100% of his buttons to 3x (300 chips). Against such a wide range, A4o has significant equity (~59%). Calling would leave me out of position with a low stack-to-pot ratio, making post-flop play difficult. Given my stack depth, the most profitable play is to move all-in (3-bet jam). This maximizes fold equity against his trash hands and realizes my equity fully when called. According to Nash equilibrium charts for Heads-Up play, A4o is a clear shove over a wide open at 16 BBs.

Pro has re-raised to 1600, leaving himself with only 100 chips. King-Eight offsuit is well ahead of a typical wide shoving range for a short-stacked opponent in heads-up play. Given the pot odds (calling 1300 to win a pot that will be 3200), I have more than enough equity to continue. I will call the 1600, and we will effectively be playing for his remaining 100 chips on any flop.

I have flopped Top Pair (Aces) on a [Tc Ah 5c] board in a large 3-bet pot. I have only 100 chips remaining (1 BB) with a pot of 3200. With an SPR of effectively 0, I am pot-committed. Checking risks giving a free card to flush draws or hands with equity against my pair. Betting 100 forces the opponent to call (which they will do with 100% of their range getting 33:1 odds) and ensures I realize my equity and protect against free draw-outs. There is no strategic merit to checking.

Pro has moved all-in for their remaining 100 chips on the flop. The current pot is 3300, making the cost to call 100. This gives me pot odds of 33:1, meaning I only need about 3% equity to make this a profitable call. Even though I only have King-high on an Ace-high board, I must call with any two cards at this price to see the runout and potentially eliminate my opponent.

I hold A9o on the button with a 33.5 BB stack. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up poker, well within the top range of hands. My opponent, Flash, has a large chip lead but tends to defend the big blind by calling rather than 3-betting aggressively against my opens. A min-raise to 200 is a standard play here to build the pot for value while maintaining pot control relative to my stack depth. It allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or have position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four of diamonds. The opponent (Pro) has a relatively short stack of 3,200 (32 BB) compared to my 16,500. Pro has raised to 200 (a min-raise). I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, giving me 3:1 odds. J4s is a reasonable hand to defend in this spot, especially with the chip lead, as it has good playability on many boards. I will call to see the flop.

I have Jack high with a backdoor diamond flush draw on a King-high board. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor to see how they respond to this board texture, which favors a pre-flop raiser's range. My hand has very little equity, so I am looking to see a cheap turn or fold to significant pressure.

I hold A9o on a K-T-5 rainbow board. While this board favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, the opponent (Flash) has demonstrated a very high frequency of check-raising flops, including on K-high textures (Hands #42, #44). With only Ace-high and very weak backdoor straight potential, my hand has some showdown value but cannot withstand a check-raise. Betting would likely fold out hands I am already beating while isolating me against better hands or inducing a bluff-raise I must fold to. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot small, and potentially call a bet on the turn or river if the opponent is bluffing, or hit an Ace/Nine to win. It effectively neutralizes the opponent's aggressive check-raise strategy.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a card that generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range. After both players checked the flop, I still have no made hand and very little equity with Jack-high. I will check to see how the opponent reacts; I am not interested in bluffing into a short stack on a board that connects well with their likely range.

Turn is the Ace of Spades, making the board K-T-5-A. This card significantly improves my hand to top pair. However, the Ace also heavily favors my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, making it a 'scare card' for the opponent. If I bet now, I risk folding out hands I beat (like Kx or Tx) that might fear I have the Ace. Furthermore, QJ completes a straight, and while unlikely, checking back ensures I don't face a check-raise in a spot where I'm either way ahead or way behind. By checking, I under-represent my hand strength, potentially inducing the opponent to bluff the river or call a value bet on the river with a King that would have folded to a turn bet. Pot control is also beneficial given my 32 BB stack.

The board is King-Ten-Five-Ace-Five. I have Jack-high. Pre-flop, the opponent (Pro) raised, but then checked both the flop and the turn. This suggests they likely don't have a strong King or Ace, but they could easily have a Ten, a small pocket pair, or a better high card like Queen-high. Since the pot is small (400 chips) and I have no showdown value with Jack-high, I could consider a bluff, but given the board contains three high cards (A, K, T), it is very likely Pro has some piece of it or a better unpaired hand they will not fold for a small bet. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown or simply forfeit the small pot if they bet, preserving my large chip lead.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker on a K-T-5-A-5 board. After raising pre-flop, I checked back both the flop and turn to pot-control against Flash, who has a high frequency of aggressive check-raises. The river pairs the 5 and Flash checks a third time. While I have a strong hand, betting for value is thin because worse hands (like Kx) are unlikely to call with the Ace on board, perceiving my range to be Ace-heavy after the check-back line. Furthermore, betting opens me up to a check-raise bluff, which would put me in a difficult spot given the board texture (QJ straight possibility) and Flash's aggressive history. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity and win the pot against weaker pairs or air without risking a fold to a re-raise.

I am on the button with 4d 5c. While this is a weak hand, I have established a consistent strategy of applying pressure from the button with 3x raises. Pro has a shorter stack of 35 BBs, and maintaining my aggressive image is important to keep them on the defensive and win the blinds. If Pro re-raises, I can easily fold this hand without much loss.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 8c, facing a 3x raise to 300 from the Button (Flash). My stack is 35 BB. The pot odds are 2:1, requiring ~33% equity to call, but my hand is an off-suit disconnected low hand (bottom 10-15% of hands). Being out of position with such poor playability against a larger stack is a losing proposition. Folding is the standard decision.

I hold 2d 3s (23 offsuit), which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having the advantage of position as the Button/Small Blind, the hand has very low equity and poor playability. My opponent 'Flash' has a massive chip lead (164 BBs vs my 34.5 BBs) and has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind frequently against raises. Engaging in the pot with this hand would likely bleed chips unnecessarily. The standard and correct play is to fold and wait for a better hand to contest the pot.

I have been dealt Ace-King of Spades, which is a premium starting hand. I am on the button with a significant chip lead over my opponent, Pro, who has a relatively short stack of 3350 chips (33.5 BB). My goal is to maximize value while putting pressure on the short stack. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size from the button, and with a hand as strong as AKs, I am looking to build a pot and am more than willing to call a shove if Pro decides to commit their remaining chips.

I am in the Big Blind with J6s, facing a 3x open (300 chips) from the Button (Flash). My stack is 33.5 BBs. Flash has been raising frequently, indicating a wide range. I am getting pot odds of 200 to call into a total pot of 600 (33% equity required). J6s has roughly 40-44% equity against a standard wide button opening range. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the hand is strong enough to defend rather than fold, as folding here would mean surrendering my blind too often. A 3-bet is risky with this hand strength and stack depth. Therefore, calling and playing a flop is the standard and most EV+ line.

On a dry, low board of 8-4-3 rainbow, I have Jack-high with backdoor flush and straight possibilities. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is not recommended here as the board texture doesn't heavily favor my range over the opener's, and my specific hand is too weak to build a pot but has enough showdown value potential (if the action goes check-check) or bluff-catching potential later to warrant keeping the pot small. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have two overcards (Ace and King) on a very dry and disconnected board. My opponent checked to me, which is expected. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure on my opponent's short stack and potentially take the pot down now. If they call, I still have significant equity with my overcards and backdoor possibilities.

On a flop of 8-4-3 rainbow, I hold Jack-high (Jh6h). While I have a backdoor flush draw and some backdoor straight potential (a 5 would give me an open-ended straight draw), I currently have no pair and no immediate draw. The opponent bets half-pot (300 into 600). Being out of position with just Jack-high, realizing my equity is difficult, and calling just to 'float' with this specific hand is a losing long-term play given the stack depth (SPR ~2.4). I need to defend my blinds, but I should do so with pairs or stronger draws (like 56, 76, or perhaps hands with two overcards). This hand is too weak to continue against a half-pot c-bet.

I hold 9c 5d in the Small Blind (Button) with a stack of 31 BBs. This is a trash hand (unconnected, offsuit, low ranks) with very poor playability and equity. While heads-up requires playing a wide range, 95o falls into the bottom percentage of hands that are generally negative EV to open, even from the button. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Js3s. With a massive chip lead over the opponent who is down to 3,000 chips (30 BB), I will continue my aggressive strategy by raising to 300. This puts pressure on the BB to defend with a marginal hand or fold, and Js3s has decent playability if called.

I hold a premium hand (Ad Kd) in the Big Blind with 30bb (3000 chips). The opponent on the Button has raised to 3x (300). While calling is an option to trap and play a smaller pot, being out of position suggests aggressive action is preferable to define ranges, build the pot for value, or take it down pre-flop. My previous play history shows a pattern of large 3-bets (to 1100 or more) with strong hands (e.g., Hand #10 with JJ, Hand #40 with AKo) and even some speculative ones. A 3-bet to 1100 is roughly 3.5x the open, creating a low SPR on the flop which simplifies my post-flop decision-making (easy shove on most flops). This size is consistent with my established 'Pro' strategy.

I am currently on the button with a massive chip lead (16,600 vs 2,000). I opened for 300 with Js3s, and the opponent 3-bet to 1,100. Since the opponent only has 900 chips remaining behind, this 3-bet effectively commits him to the pot. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (800 to call into a pot of 1,400), Js3s is significantly behind a typical short-stack 3-betting range. I have a huge structural advantage in this match, and there is no need to take a high-variance flip or be an underdog in a pot that would allow the opponent to double up. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my chip lead to finish the match.

Current hand is 3c Jd (J3 offsuit). This is a very weak hand, typically in the bottom 15% of starting hands. With a stack of 33.5 BB, I need to pick better spots to enter the pot. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position effectively if called (though technically in position post-flop, the hand strength is too low to sustain aggression). The standard GTO play and exploitative play for this stack depth is to fold.

I am on the button with 9d5c. My opponent, Pro, is very short-stacked with only 3250 chips (32.5 BB). In this heads-up situation, I want to keep applying pressure, but 95o is a relatively weak hand. However, given Pro's tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands and their current stack size, a standard raise puts them in a difficult position. I will raise to 300 to try and take down the blinds or see a flop in position.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd4d facing a standard 3x open raise from the Button (Flash). My hand, K4 suited, is well within the standard defense range against a late-position open, especially given the 3:1 pot odds I am getting to call (200 to win 600). Flash is opening frequently as the chip leader, suggesting a wide range. My stack is 32.5 BBs, so I can afford to see a flop. 3-betting is too risky with this hand strength and stack depth, so a flat call is the most prudent action to realize my equity.

I hold the nut flush draw (Kd on Ad-high board) with Kd4d on a flop of 5h-8d-Ad. The Ace hits the pre-flop raiser's range hard, making a donk bet unlikely to generate folds from better hands (top pair) and likely to fold out worse hands that might bluff. Checking is the standard play from the Big Blind to the pre-flop aggressor. If the opponent bets, I have a very strong hand to continue with (check-call or potentially check-raise), but checking first allows me to see their action and keep the pot manageable relative to my 30BB stack.

I have bottom pair on an Ace-high board after raising pre-flop. My opponent checked to me, which is a standard move. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent the Ace and attempt to take down the pot immediately. This also provides information; if Pro calls or raises, I can be reasonably sure my pair of fives is behind.

I hold the nut flush draw (Kd on Ad-8d-5h board) in a heads-up pot. Facing a half-pot c-bet of 300 into 600, I have excellent pot odds (3:1) to continue. While a check-raise is a viable aggressive option with the nut flush draw, the presence of the Ace on the board hits the opponent's opening range hard, meaning he is less likely to fold top pair. Calling keeps his bluffing range wide and manages the pot size relative to my 30BB stack, avoiding a situation where I might face a shove and have to call off my tournament life as an underdog.

I hold the nut flush draw (Kd on Ad-8d-5h-8c board). Since I am out of position in the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is standard. The Turn card (8c) pairs the board, which might slow down the opponent if they don't have an 8 or a strong Ace. Leading out (donking) is risky as it exposes me to raises that could force me to fold my equity or commit my stack with just a draw. Checking allows me to potentially see a free River or evaluate a bet from the opponent based on pot odds.

The board has paired on the turn with the Eight of Clubs, making the board Ace-high with trip Eights possible. I currently hold bottom pair (Fives). My opponent, Pro, has checked again. Since the board is now quite dangerous and my hand has some showdown value but is unlikely to improve further or force a better hand to fold without significant risk, I will check back to keep the pot small and see a free river card. This also protects my stack against a potential check-raise from a short-stacked opponent who might be trapping with an Ace or an Eight.

I hold Kd4d on a board of 5h-8d-Ad-8c-4h. My hand has improved to Two Pair (Eights and Fours) with an Ace kicker on the board (Best 5: 8-8-4-4-A). The opponent raised pre-flop, bet the flop, but checked back the turn. This check-back suggests they likely do not have a strong Ace or an Eight, capping their range to weak Aces, mid-pairs, or missed high-card draws. My hand beats all unpaired hands (high cards) but loses to any pocket pair 55+ or an Ace/Eight. Betting here has no value because worse hands (unpaired high cards) will fold, and better hands (Ax, pairs) will call. Checking is the optimal line to try and get to showdown cheaply against the portion of their range I beat, or to evaluate a river bet.

I have a pair of fives with a nine kicker on an Ace-high board that paired Eights on the turn. After my opponent called the flop continuation bet and we both checked the turn, the river is a brick. My hand has decent showdown value in a heads-up pot, as my opponent could be holding a missed diamond draw or a weaker hand. Betting here likely only gets called by better hands (Aces, Eights, or better Fives) and won't force a better hand to fold given the stack sizes. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown without risking more chips against a potential trap.

I hold King-Queen suited (Qs Ks) on the button with a 27bb stack. This is a premium hand in heads-up poker, well above the median hand strength. My standard opening strategy from the button has been a min-raise to 200, which I have done with various hands (e.g., Hand #71, #23). Raising allows me to build the pot with a strong hand while in position and maintains balance with my wider opening range. Open-shoving is unnecessary at this stack depth (27bb) as I want to invite action from worse hands. Limping is passive and allows the opponent to see a flop for free with random holdings. Therefore, a standard raise to 200 is the best action.

I have Ace-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. Pro is playing with a very short stack (25.5 BB) and raised to 2x from the button. A5o is a strong heads-up hand with an Ace blocker. I will 3-bet to 600 to put pressure on his small stack and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate him.

I hold KsQs on the button with approximately 25.5 big blinds. After I min-raised to 200, the opponent (Flash) 3-bet to 600. KQs is a premium hand in heads-up play, blocking AA, KK, QQ, and AK. I have excellent pot odds to call (400 to win a total pot of 1200, requiring 33% equity), and my hand has significantly better equity than that against a typical 3-bet range. With a stack-to-pot ratio of roughly 1.8 post-flop (2150 stack / 1200 pot), I will be in position with a hand that flops well. Shoving (4-betting all-in) is a viable option at this stack depth but risks isolating me against a stronger range while folding out hands I dominate. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and leverages my position.

On the flop of Qc-5c-9h, I have middle pair (fives) with an Ace kicker. The opponent, Pro, has a very short stack of 2150 remaining. I will check to see how the opponent reacts. Given their stack size, if they bet, I'm likely committed to calling with middle pair in a 3-bet pot heads-up, but checking allows them to potentially bluff or check behind with weaker hands.

I hold King-Queen of Spades on a Queen-Five-Nine board with two clubs. I have Top Pair Top Kicker. The opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked to me. While the board is somewhat wet with flush and straight draws, checking back is a strong option for several reasons: 1) It controls the size of the pot in a scenario where the SPR is low (1.8), preventing me from being blown off my equity by a check-raise. 2) It protects my checking range, balancing the times I check with weaker hands. 3) It induces bluffs from an aggressive opponent who may interpret my check as weakness and fire on the turn with hands I have crushed (like lower pairs or air). 4) I am in position to re-evaluate on the turn. Although betting to deny equity to draws is viable, checking allows me to capture value from the opponent's aggressive tendencies.

I have two pair (fives and nines) with an Ace kicker. The turn card is a nine, which pairs the board. Since both players checked the flop, it is less likely that anyone is holding a strong Queen. I will check again to maintain pot control and see how the opponent reacts, potentially inducing a bluff from their short stack or taking a free river to evaluate the strength of my hand.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (KsQs) on a board of Qc-5c-9h-9d. The previous plan on the flop was to check back to control the pot and induce bluffs from the aggressive opponent, Flash. On the turn, Flash has checked again. The 9d pairs the board, which makes it less likely the opponent holds a 9 (given the pre-flop 3-bet and card removal) and counterfeits potential two-pair hands like Q5. While betting to deny equity to flush draws is a standard play, checking back here is consistent with the flop strategy to under-represent my hand strength. By checking twice, I strongly encourage the opponent to bluff the river with their missed overs or air, or to make a thin value call on the river with a hand like TT or JJ. Given the stack-to-pot ratio is just under 2, I can comfortably call a river bet or bet for value on the river if checked to again, avoiding a difficult spot if I were check-raised on the turn.

I have two pair (Nines and Fives) with an Ace kicker. The board has a pair of Nines and a potential club flush that just arrived on the river. Given the passive action on the flop and turn (both players checking), it is unlikely Pro has a Queen or a Nine. However, with Pro's short stack of 2150 and the pot at 1200, I will check to see if they want to bluff or check behind with a weaker hand. My hand is strong enough to call a reasonable bet, but checking ensures I don't over-commit if Pro was trapping with a monster or hit the flush.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (KsQs) on a board of Qc-5c-9h-9d-7c. The river 7c completes the club flush draw. My opponent, Flash, 3-bet pre-flop but has checked to me on the flop, turn, and now river.
